items.title: Jobs +172K, unemployment 4.3% — rates rip, Nasdaq -4.8%, markets now pricing rate hike
items.idea: Friday's jobs report (payrolls +172K, unemployment 4.3%) was strong enough to revive the question the market thought was retired: is the next Fed move a hike, not a cut? Nasdaq dropped 4.8% — worst session since April 2025. S&P fell 2.6%. Treasury yields surged and markets moved toward pricing a greater probability of a rate hike before year-end. The inflation dragon may not be dead: tariffs lifting costs, AI buildout driving massive capex spend, Middle East keeping energy uncertain.
items.angle: This changes the macro regime framing for the week. Rate-cut trades need to be unwound or hedged. The jobs print didn't just delay cuts — it introduced hike probability.
items.tweetA: 172K payrolls. 4.3% unemployment. This is the report that killed the rate-cut narrative and replaced it with "what if the next move is a hike." Nasdaq -4.8%, worst session since April 2025. The AI freight train just met the inflation dragon. $QQQ $SPY $TLT
items.tweetB: Everyone spent 6 months modeling rate cuts. One jobs report flipped the script. The Fed is no longer thinking about cuts — it's running the math on whether inflation needs to be forced back in the bottle. AI capex cycle + tariffs + Middle East = the inflation argument isn't dead.

charts.desc: CBOE Crude Oil ETF Volatility Index; Apollo/Torsten Slok chart on AI-driven explosion in entrepreneur count

items.source: P Equity Research / Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: KOSPI -8.8% Monday — South Korean margin debt record KRW 38T, 60+ yr-olds are 1/3 of it
items.idea: South Korea's KOSPI tumbled 8.8% Monday, with Samsung and SK Hynix leading losses as the AI semiconductor trade unwinds. The backdrop: Korean margin debt surged to a record KRW 38 trillion from KRW 25 trillion in just weeks since end of April. Investors aged 60+ now account for nearly one-third of total margin debt. Elderly Koreans have been selling insurance policies and borrowing on margin to trade memory stocks. When margin debt this concentrated unwinds, it becomes forced selling.
items.angle: This is the specific feedback loop that amplifies Korea selloffs. It's not just a tech unwind — it's a margin call cascade among retirees. Could be a signal for broader EM risk-off.
items.tweetA: South Korean margin debt hit a record KRW 38 trillion — up from KRW 25T in just weeks. One-third of it is held by people aged 60+. These are retirees, borrowing against insurance policies, to bet on Samsung and SK Hynix. When this unwinds it isn't orderly. $KOSPI $SSNLF
items.tweetB: The most crowded AI trade in the world isn't $NVDA. It's Korean retirees on margin in memory stocks. KRW 38T and counting. Monday KOSPI -8.8%. The margin call cycle in Korea is just starting.

items.title: AI is the Jevons Paradox in real time — efficiency multiplies demand, buildout phase is inflationary
items.idea: The AI boom is evolving from an earnings story into the largest capital spending cycle in modern history. Apollo's Torsten Slok documents an explosion in the number of entrepreneurs enabled by AI. This is the Jevons Paradox: greater efficiency doesn't reduce demand, it creates more. What investors model as a future disinflationary force may prove inflationary during the initial buildout phase. Lower barriers to entry → surge in startup formation → more capex demand at every layer.
items.angle: The consensus AI trade is 'AI = deflationary long-run.' This flips it: AI = inflationary short-run. Implications for rates, infrastructure plays ($EQIX, $NEE, $VST), and the Fed's reaction function.
items.tweetA: The market prices AI as a future disinflationary force. The buildout phase is the opposite. More startups, more compute, more data centers, more electricity, more infrastructure. Jevons Paradox in real time: efficiency breeds demand. Don't confuse the long-run outcome with what's happening right now.
items.tweetB: Apollo's Torsten Slok has a chart showing AI-driven explosion in number of entrepreneurs. More businesses = more demand for everything. You want deflation? Wait 10 years. Right now, AI is a massive inflationary capex cycle and the Fed is starting to see it. $NVDA $EQIX $NEE

charts.desc: Apollo/Torsten Slok: AI-Driven Explosion in Number of Entrepreneurs — strong visual worth saving

items.title: Iran attacks Israel for first time since April 8 ceasefire — Trump rushes to stop retaliation
items.idea: Iran attacked Israel on June 7 for the first time since the April 8th ceasefire. Trump immediately rushed to convince Netanyahu not to attack back. Iran sent 5 conditions for a deal, including sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz. The US explicitly rejects 2 conditions. US officials are also discussing military options against Iran on Tuesday.
items.angle: Hormuz sovereignty as a peace condition is a non-starter. This is heading toward another escalation cycle just as markets treated the conflict as priced in.
items.tweetA: Iran attacked Israel today for the first time since the April 8 ceasefire. Trump called Netanyahu immediately to prevent retaliation. One of Iran's peace conditions: recognition of Iranian sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz. They aren't negotiating peace. They're negotiating leverage. $USO $CL_F
items.tweetB: The ceasefire broke today. Iran attacked Israel. The US says military options are on the table for Tuesday. And Iran's demand list includes Hormuz sovereignty. The 'this is priced in' crowd needs to check their work. $XLE $VLO $MPC $USO

items.title: Iran turning the Strait of Hormuz into a geopolitical toll road — collecting millions from transiting vessels
items.idea: Tehran is collecting millions of dollars from vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, turning one of the world's most critical trade routes into a geopolitical toll road. One-third of global oil flows through this chokepoint. Dark Side of the Boom frames oil's behavior as pricing insurance, not Armageddon: every barrel above pre-conflict levels represents a premium for the probability that one exchange escapes containment.
items.angle: If Hormuz transit fees become normalized, it permanently changes the cost structure of global energy trade. Bullish for LNG alternatives, pipelines, and energy security capex.
items.tweetA: Iran is charging ships a toll to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. Not a hypothetical — it's happening now. One-third of global oil flows there. What do you call it when a hostile state starts taxing your energy supply chain? The market calls it an insurance premium on crude. $USO $XOM $XLE
items.tweetB: Buried in Dark Side of the Boom: Tehran is 'collecting millions from vessels transiting Hormuz.' Oil isn't pricing Armageddon — it's pricing insurance. The real question is what happens when one of these incidents escapes containment.

items.title: Xi in Pyongyang June 8-9 (first time since 2019) — Beijing forced to reinsert itself as Kim outgrew China's leverage
items.idea: Xi Jinping is visiting North Korea June 8-9. The real read: Beijing is being FORCED to physically reinsert itself because Kim monetized the Russia war and reduced China's monopoly leverage. North Korea's Russia channel gives Kim weapons-for-cash, diplomatic cover — breaking China's old leverage model. Xi cannot let Putin look like Kim's senior patron. This visit is about repricing access and restoring hierarchy in the China-Russia-North Korea triangle.
items.angle: The China-NK relationship is weakening while Russia-NK is strengthening. Implications for Korean Peninsula risk, USFK posture, and Japan-South Korea military cooperation.
items.tweetA: Xi is visiting Pyongyang for the first time since 2019. The conventional read: China supports North Korea. The actual read: Beijing is LOSING leverage. Kim monetized the Russia war — got weapons, cash, and diplomatic cover — and doesn't need Chinese fuel access to stay obedient anymore.
items.tweetB: China-Russia-North Korea triangle is restructuring. Putin is becoming Kim's functional patron. Xi can't let that stand — hence the personal visit. This isn't about denuclearization. It's about who Kim answers to when Moscow and Beijing disagree.

items.title: NK nuclear fuel capacity claimed 2x; 'irreversible' declared before Xi arrived — nuclear file is now a balance-sheet asset
items.idea: Kim Jong Un toured a newly inaugurated nuclear-fuel facility June 3, claimed output capacity doubled (2x). Kim Yo-jong declared the nuclear program 'irreversible' before Xi arrived — timed as leverage. The nuclear file has moved from bargaining chip to balance-sheet asset. By unveiling enrichment capacity before the summit, Kim converts nuclear expansion into a sunk-cost position. Watch the June 9 summit readout: 'denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula' vs. 'peace, stability, legitimate security concerns.' The word choice is everything.
items.angle: If 'denuclearization' disappears from the June 9 readout, Beijing has accepted nuclear North Korea. That's a 7-year arms race signal for South Korea and Japan.
items.tweetA: North Korea doubled its nuclear fuel output capacity and declared the program 'irreversible' — before Xi even landed. Kim isn't negotiating. He's normalizing. Every new facility is a sunk-cost argument: you can't roll this back. Watch the June 9 communiqué for whether 'denuclearization' disappears.
items.tweetB: The tell tomorrow: does the Xi-Kim communiqué say 'denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula' or just 'peace, stability, and legitimate security concerns'? That single phrase is everything. One means Beijing still pretends. The other means they've accepted a nuclear North Korea.

items.title: China-South Korea flight expansion (608→664 passenger, 54→68 air freight) simultaneous with NK summit
items.idea: While Xi visits Pyongyang, China and South Korea are expanding commercial flight rights: passenger 608→664/week, air freight 54→68/week. Beijing is running two tracks: managing North Korea while deepening economic ties with Seoul. The real lever of Beijing's NK influence isn't diplomacy — it's the Dandong-Sinuiju border valve. Enforcement speed of customs, fuel movements, and rail freight determines Pyongyang's cash flow, not summit language.
items.angle: China-Seoul economic rapprochement happening during the NK summit signals Beijing doesn't want Pyongyang visit to damage bilateral trade. Two-track geopolitics in Northeast Asia.
items.tweetA: Same week Xi visits Pyongyang: China and South Korea quietly expand commercial flights. Passenger routes 608→664, air freight 54→68 per week. Beijing is running two tracks: managing Kim on one hand, deepening Seoul economic ties on the other. Geopolitical multitasking.
items.tweetB: The NK summit will dominate headlines. The China-South Korea flight expansion won't. But that's the signal: Beijing doesn't want the Pyongyang visit to damage Seoul economic ties. The China-Korea relationship is being deepened simultaneously.

items.title: Broadcom maintained AI guidance (not raised) — 'meeting = downgrade' for hypergrowth-priced sector
items.idea: Broadcom reported record Q2 revenue $22.2B (+48% YoY), guided Q3 to $29.4B, and maintained long-term AI semiconductor revenue target >$100B by FY2027 instead of raising it. Market dropped stock 7.9%, Macquarie downgraded to Neutral. For a sector priced on hypergrowth, maintaining guidance = de facto downgrade. Investors had embedded more aggressive AI upside than management endorsed. Marvell fell 16.7% (had surged 100%+ in under a month), SMH -9.2%.
items.angle: This sets the template for AI earnings season: any company that meets but doesn't beat elevated expectations risks a Broadcom-style selloff. The AI premium is being stress-tested.
items.tweetA: Broadcom Q2: +48% YoY, record revenue, strong guidance. Market reaction: -8%, sector rout. Why? Because when you're priced for infinity, 'meeting expectations' is a downgrade. SMH -9.2%. Marvell -16.7%. The AI trade is being re-priced from narrative to proof-of-execution. $AVGO $SMH
items.tweetB: >$100B AI revenue by FY2027 is Broadcom's target. They didn't raise it. That's the entire problem. Stocks priced for 'beats every quarter' repriced instantly when the beat didn't come. This is the new math of the AI sector.

charts.desc: Charts inside article worth opening for sector breakdown

items.title: SMH RSI at 29.67 (6-month oversold), hit 4x before — but this time followed a larger drawdown
items.idea: SMH's 6-month RSI hit 29.67, reached only 4 times previously in this period, each time leading to a sharp bounce. BUT: the prior instances didn't follow a week with a 9%+ single-day loss. SMH is still up 58% YTD even after Friday's -9.2%. The ETF's concentration in 3 mega-caps (Broadcom, NVIDIA, TSMC) means single-company guidance drives the whole index. Two competing signals: historical RSI says buy the dip; Broadcom guidance reset says AI multiple de-rating may have further to run.
items.angle: Oversold bounce setup vs. fundamental re-rating cycle. The RSI signals say cover shorts. The guidance reset says the AI premium hasn't fully normalized yet. Position sizing becomes critical.
items.tweetA: SMH's 6-month RSI hit 29.67. That level triggered a sharp bounce all 4 prior times in this cycle. But the prior bounces didn't follow a week where the sector lost 9%+ in a single day. The setup is there — so is the counterargument. $SMH $AVGO $NVDA
items.tweetB: SMH is still up 58% year-to-date even after Friday's destruction. Think about what that means: there's still enormous multiple expansion baked in for AI semis. The bounce thesis competes with the de-rating thesis.

items.title: Saylor sold BTC — broke 'perpetual buyer' narrative; $5B in spot ETF outflows over past month
items.idea: Strategy (Saylor) sold a portion of Bitcoin — small in size, enormous in signal. The trade had been that Strategy NEVER sells. Once price sensitivity was shown, the option value of 'permanent bid' evaporated. Three demand pillars collapsed simultaneously: (1) corporate treasuries shown to be conditional buyers; (2) ETF flows net negative ~$5B over past month, $396.6M single-day outflow; (3) discretionary traders forced to reduce risk. BTC wicked below $60K from $77K (per Seven C). GBTC -5.15%, ETHE -11.21%.
items.angle: The reflexive cycle is now in reverse: lower BTC prices impair Strategy's collateral → tighter financing → higher probability of further sales → more selling.
items.tweetA: Saylor sold. Small amount. Doesn't matter. The trade was that Strategy NEVER sells. Once you show price sensitivity, the option value of 'permanent bid' evaporates. Spot ETFs shed ~$5B in BTC over the past month. The institutional narrative was the bull case. Now watch what happens without it. $MSTR $GBTC
items.tweetB: Three demand pillars for Bitcoin cracking: (1) corporate treasuries shown to be price-sensitive, not unconditional. (2) ETF flows flipped — $396M single-day outflow. (3) Discretionary funds reducing risk. BTC wicked below $60K from $77K. The reflexive cycle is in reverse. $BTC $MSTR

items.title: Lululemon Q2 EPS guidance $1.76-1.81 vs $2.71 expected — premium consumer discretionary softening
items.idea: Lululemon missed Q1 EPS slightly but issued devastating guidance: Q2 EPS $1.76-$1.81 vs Street at $2.71 (35%+ miss). Full-year EPS cut from $12.10-$12.30 to $10.95-$11.15. Management cited 'softer consumer response, product and traffic challenges.' Lululemon targets high-income consumers who were supposed to be recession-proof. When even the affluent demographic cracks, the 'narrow expansion' thesis gets further confirmation.
items.angle: The economy is being carried by AI capex + infrastructure + top-1% consumers. Lululemon says that third pillar is softening. Bearish for $XLY, confirms the narrow-expansion thesis.
items.tweetA: Lululemon Q2 EPS guidance: $1.76. Street was at $2.71. That's not a miss — that's a collapse. Full-year EPS cut from $12.30 to $11.05 at the midpoint. High-income consumers who were supposed to be recession-proof are pulling back. $LULU $XLY
items.tweetB: When the yoga pants company misses forward EPS by 35%+, you don't just have a Lululemon problem. You have a 'premium consumer discretionary is softening' signal. The AI economy is doing great. Everything else is getting squeezed. $LULU $NKE $XLY

items.title: Berkshire buys Taylor Morrison for $6.8B — Greg Abel's debut deal, $397B cash pile beginning to deploy
items.idea: Berkshire acquiring Taylor Morrison at $72.50/share (24% premium), $6.8B equity, $8.5B enterprise value. Greg Abel's first multibillion-dollar acquisition post-Buffett. Berkshire already owns Clayton Homes (manufactured) + BHHS (brokerage) — Taylor Morrison adds site-built homes. Buffett: 'Greg did it faster than I could have, smoother... He has launched.' Berkshire still holds $397B in cash. $6.8B barely dents the pile.
items.angle: Abel is consolidating a housing conglomerate. $6.8B is the opening move. The housing thesis: Sun Belt + Mountain West + demographic tailwinds + Berkshire's long-duration capital.
items.tweetA: Berkshire has $397B in cash. They just spent $6.8B on a homebuilder. Buffett: 'Greg did it faster than I could have, smoother... He has launched.' This is Abel's coming-out party — and a 24% premium signals conviction. The real question: what does he buy next? $BRK.B
items.tweetB: Berkshire already owns manufactured housing (Clayton), brokerage (HomeServices). Now adds site-built homes (Taylor Morrison). Abel is building a housing conglomerate piece by piece. $397B cash, 24% premium, barely dents the balance sheet. This is chapter 1.

items.title: Multiple US states preparing lawsuit to block $110B WBD-Paramount Skydance deal
items.idea: Multiple US states are preparing a lawsuit challenging the $110B Warner Bros. Discovery acquisition by Paramount Skydance. Federal approval had been anticipated, but state AG involvement represents a new front in M&A antitrust enforcement. WBD shares fell 2.81% on the news.
items.angle: State-level antitrust enforcement is the new frontier for mega-deals. If this succeeds, every media/tech consolidation will need to stress-test state-level opposition in addition to federal.
items.tweetA: Multiple US states are preparing to sue to block the $110 billion Warner Bros.-Paramount Skydance deal. The deal everyone thought was done now faces a state-level antitrust gauntlet. Media consolidation in the streaming era just got significantly harder. $WBD $PARA
items.tweetB: State AGs are becoming the new front line for M&A antitrust. After DOJ cleared the path, states are moving in. If this works against WBD-Paramount, every mega-deal in media and tech will need to price state-level opposition. Different kind of risk.

items.title: VIX spiked 39%+ in single day — TMAD hedged since last Sunday, put spreads up 50%; SpaceX IPO + FOMC still ahead
items.idea: VIX surged more than 39% in a single session on Friday — a regime-shift signal. TMAD had been positioned for this since last Sunday, flagging market as 'looking fragile' and expecting a June pullback. Put spreads currently up nearly 50%. Crucially: the two catalysts they flagged — SpaceX IPO and FOMC — haven't happened yet. Jobs data was the trigger, but the larger positioning events are still ahead.
items.angle: If SpaceX IPO and FOMC are still event risk, the current selloff may not be complete. Two known liquidity-consuming events in a freshly spooked market.
items.tweetA: VIX spiked 39% in a single session Friday. That's not noise — that's a regime signal. TMAD had been hedged since last Sunday. Their put spreads are up 50%. Crucially: the two catalysts they actually flagged — SpaceX IPO and FOMC — haven't happened yet.
items.tweetB: The jobs data triggered Friday's selloff. But the bigger event risk is still ahead: SpaceX IPO (major liquidity draw) + FOMC (rate hike probability repriced). VIX at elevated levels with known catalysts upcoming is a specific setup. Worth having options exposure here.

items.title: Trump meeting AI leaders at White House this week — exploring equity stake for Americans in AI firms
items.idea: Trump is meeting with AI leaders (likely Altman, Amodei, others) at the White House this week. The framing: equity stake in AI companies for Americans to participate financially. Multiple interpretations: positive (democratized access), bearish (regulatory capture), alarming (quasi-nationalization under national security frame). The outcome could affect how AI companies do future financing and their operating independence.
items.angle: Government equity stakes in private AI companies is a genuinely novel governance structure. Could affect AI company IPO timelines, financing, and product independence.
items.tweetA: Trump is meeting AI leaders at the White House this week. The stated goal: equity stakes for Americans in AI companies. The unstated goals range from democratizing AI returns to de facto government control over the most important technology sector in history. This meeting matters more than it's getting credit for. $MSFT $META $GOOGL $NVDA
items.tweetB: US government taking equity stakes in AI companies. This is either (a) financial innovation, (b) regulatory capture, or (c) quasi-nationalization disguised as investor inclusion. Whatever it is, it changes the AI corporate governance calculus.

items.title: Claude Opus 4.8 found Zcash's infinite-money bug in 28 hours — bug survived years of human audits since 2022
items.idea: Security researcher Taylor Hornby used Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.8 in a custom audit-agent framework to find a critical bug in Zcash's Orchard shielded pool. Bug present since Orchard launched May 31, 2022 — allowed attacker to generate counterfeit ZEC infinitely without visible on-chain signature. Hornby's PoC doubled a note's balance repeatedly until test wallet held 10 million ZEC, all proofs valid. AI found in 28 hours what years of human audits missed. Emergency soft fork June 2, network upgrade June 3. 2nd critical Zcash bug in 90 days.
items.angle: Inflection point for security auditing. Every protocol with novel ZK circuits should ask: has an AI looked at this? The gap between AI-audited and human-audited cryptographic code is widening fast.
items.tweetA: Claude Opus 4.8 found a bug in Zcash that allowed unlimited coin counterfeiting in the shielded pool. It had been live since May 2022. Survived 4+ years of human audits. AI found it in 28 hours. The question: what else is hiding in crypto code that humans have missed? $ZEC $ETH
items.tweetB: The Zcash finding is a preview of what AI auditing will do to blockchain security. Every protocol with complex ZK circuits now faces a simple question: has an AI reviewed this? The delta between AI-audited and human-audited cryptographic code is becoming too large to ignore.

items.title: Helion Energy breaks ground on first commercial fusion plant — $465M Series G, $15.5B valuation, Microsoft 2028 PPA
items.idea: Helion Energy raised $465M Series G at $15.5B valuation ($1.5B total raised). Building Orion, the first commercial fusion power plant, in Washington state. Contracted to deliver electricity to Microsoft starting 2028 — the first binding PPA in commercial fusion history. In February, Helion's Polaris machine achieved 150 million °C plasma (10x hotter than Sun's core) using deuterium-tritium fuel. The 2028 timeline is the most aggressive in the industry by roughly 5 years.
items.angle: Fusion transitioning from 'always 30 years away' to 'under construction with a buyer.' The Microsoft PPA is the biggest credibility milestone in fusion history. Long-run implications for nuclear and grid.
items.tweetA: Helion just broke ground on the first commercial fusion power plant. $465M Series G. $15.5B valuation. Microsoft is contracted to buy electricity starting 2028. They already hit 150M°C plasma — 10x hotter than the Sun's core. Most aggressive timeline in the industry by 5 years.
items.tweetB: The Sam Altman-backed fusion company is now $15.5B and has a building going up. When you have a binding power purchase agreement with Microsoft before your plant is built, you're no longer a research project. Fusion is transitioning from 'maybe someday' to 'scheduled delivery date.'

items.title: Thucydides Trap triggered by middle powers, not inevitability — Xi mentioned it by name to Trump in Beijing
items.idea: Peter Frankopan wrote for The Spectator that Athens-Sparta war turned to confrontation not because great powers are destined to fight, but because of middle powers. Xi Jinping mentioned the 'Thucydides Trap' by name when meeting Trump in Beijing last month — making this framework live in current diplomacy. The 'middle powers' today: Saudi Arabia, India, Turkey, South Korea, Israel, Iran, Pakistan, Indonesia.
items.angle: The Thucydides Trap debate is happening at the head-of-state level. Middle powers — Saudi Arabia, India especially — are increasingly the ones whose decisions will determine whether US-China rivalry stays cold or turns hot.
items.tweetA: Xi mentioned the 'Thucydides Trap' by name to Trump in Beijing last month. Peter Frankopan's revision: Athens and Sparta didn't fight because of inevitable great power rivalry. They fought because middle powers made choices that forced the confrontation. In 2026, those middle powers are Saudi Arabia, India, Turkey, South Korea. Watch them.
items.tweetB: The conventional Thucydides Trap: rising power + established power = war inevitable. Frankopan's revision: it's never about the two big players. It's about which third party lights the fuse. In 2026, the list of potential fuse-lighters has never been longer.

items.title: Three strange things: unusual oil/China, odd Central Asia nuclear deal, South Asia cyber boost (article paywalled)
items.idea: Peter Frankopan flags three topics: (1) unusual goings-on in the oil market involving China and the Gulf; (2) an odd nuclear power deal in Central Asia — likely Chinese or Russian firms expanding nuclear into Kazakhstan or Uzbekistan; (3) a development boosting malign cyber operations in South Asia. Full content behind paywall. Frankopan's 'strange things' tend to be 6-18 month early indicators.
items.angle: An unusual Central Asia nuclear deal could affect uranium supply chains. South Asia cyber developments often connect to broader information warfare trends worth tracking.
items.tweetA: Peter Frankopan flags three 'strange things' this week: unusual oil dynamics involving China, an odd nuclear power deal in Central Asia, and something boosting malign cyber operations in South Asia. The last time he flagged something unusual in Central Asia energy, it mattered 6 months later.
items.tweetB: When a historian of the Silk Road says there are three unusual things in energy, nuclear, and cyber this week — you pay attention. Frankopan surfaces what becomes market-relevant before it shows up in financial media.

items.title: VaR shock mechanics: Momentum reversed from +5% to -50bps; Fear-Greed at 'Fear' while S&P only 300bps from ATH
items.idea: Eliant frames Friday as a VaR shock, not a fundamental repricing. The Momentum factor was up more than 5% on the week before reversing to -50bps by Friday's close — violent single-day factor unwind. QQQ ended the week -450bps, Dow -21bps. Best-performing factors: Value and Low-Volatility. % Stocks Above 20D at 46%, encroaching oversold. Fear-Greed Index back in 'Fear' despite S&P being only ~300bps below ATH. Eliant: 'markets far from true euphoria or positioning extremes typically associated with major tops.'
items.angle: VaR shock + factor unwind + Fear-Greed in 'Fear' while near ATH = historically a buy-the-dip setup, not a distribution top. But macro catalyst (hike fears) + upcoming SpaceX IPO + FOMC add uncertainty to timing.
items.tweetA: The real Friday story: the Momentum factor reversed from +5%+ on the week to negative 50bps by close. That's a systematic factor unwind, not fundamental selling. VaR shock mechanics. Fear-Greed back in 'Fear' while S&P is 300bps from ATH. I've seen this setup before. $SPY $QQQ
items.tweetB: Fear-Greed Index in 'Fear' with S&P 300bps from all-time highs. Momentum just had a violent single-day reversal. Value and Low-Vol outperforming. % stocks above 20D at 46%. This is the anatomy of a shakeout, not a distribution top. The question is how long before the dip-buyers arrive.

charts.desc: Historical Fear-Greed Index overlaid with S&P 500; STD channel charts on SPY/QQQ/IWM/DJIA — multiple charts worth opening
